source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 11.3 Systemd (p. 201)

options: A service manager for Linux and the default initialization system since RedHat/CentOS 7
questions.why: systemd is the service manager and has been the default init system since RHEL/CentOS 7.

questions.q: Which design goals drove the development of systemd?
options: Compatibility with old SysV init scripts, plus parallel startup, on-demand daemon activation, snapshots and dependency management
Running only one service at a time
questions.why: systemd keeps SysV compatibility while adding parallel start, on-demand activation, snapshots and dependency handling.

questions.q: Which file extension identifies a systemd SERVICE unit, and which a TARGET unit?
options: .service for a system service; .target for a group of units
.target for a service; .service for a group
.d for a service; .cfg for a target
questions.why: A .service unit is a system service; a .target unit groups other units (there are many other types: mount, socket, timer, ...).

questions.q: How does systemd's socket activation help when restarting a service?
options: It holds the listening socket and queues incoming messages, so a restart loses no messages
It disables the network during the restart
questions.why: systemd creates the listening sockets and passes them to services; the socket stays available and messages queue, so a restart drops nothing.

questions.q: What is the default timeout applied to service unit operations?
questions.why: A 5-minute default timeout stops a malfunctioning service from freezing the system.
